content.text: The sentence-transformers library is the standard Python tool for computing embeddings locally using pre-trained transformer models. Unlike provider APIs, it runs entirely on your own hardware — no network call, no per-token billing, no data leaving your infrastructure. It wraps HuggingFace transformers with a clean interface: load a model, call encode(), get normalized vectors. The same models that power cross-lingual retrieval, semantic search, and clustering are available with a single pip install.

attrs.alt: sentence-transformers architecture: input strings flow through tokenizer, transformer encoder, and mean pooling to produce fixed-size vectors. Popular models: all-MiniLM-L6-v2, all-mpnet-base-v2, multilingual-e5-large, bge-m3. Bottom shows asymmetric encoding with query/passage prefixes and local vs. API decision guide.

content.text:
pip install sentence-transformers
# For GPU support:
pip install sentence-transformers torch --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cu121

from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
import numpy as np

# First call downloads the model from HuggingFace Hub and caches it locally
# (~90 MB for MiniLM, ~420 MB for mpnet)
model = SentenceTransformer("all-MiniLM-L6-v2")

# Encode a list of sentences — returns a numpy array of shape (N, dim)
sentences = [
    "Machine learning models learn patterns from data.",
    "Deep neural networks have many layers.",
    "The weather today is sunny and warm.",
]
vectors = model.encode(sentences)  # shape: (3, 384)
print(vectors.shape)  # (3, 384)
print(type(vectors))  # <class 'numpy.ndarray'>

# Convert to list for storage or API responses
vectors_list = vectors.tolist()  # list[list[float]]

sentence-transformers automatically detects CUDA GPUs. You can also specify the device explicitly. For macOS with Apple Silicon, MPS (Metal Performance Shaders) provides significant speedups over CPU.

import torch
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer

def best_device() -> str:
    if torch.cuda.is_available():
        return "cuda"
    if torch.backends.mps.is_available():
        return "mps"
    return "cpu"

device = best_device()
model = SentenceTransformer("all-MiniLM-L6-v2", device=device)
print(f"Running on: {device}")

# Approximate throughput (all-MiniLM-L6-v2, 384-dim):
# CPU (4-core): ~500-1000 sentences/second
# GPU (RTX 3090): ~15000-25000 sentences/second
# Apple M2 MPS:  ~3000-5000 sentences/second
#
# For all-mpnet-base-v2 (110M params, 768-dim): roughly 3-5x slower
# For multilingual-e5-large (560M params): roughly 10-15x slower than MiniLM
method handles batching internally. You pass the full list and set
to control how many sentences are encoded per forward pass. Larger batches are faster but use more GPU/CPU memory.

from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer

model = SentenceTransformer("all-MiniLM-L6-v2")

# Encoding 100k documents
documents = [f"Document {i}: some content about topic {i % 100}." for i in range(100_000)]

# batch_size controls the mini-batch size internally
# show_progress_bar prints a tqdm progress bar
vectors = model.encode(
    documents,
    batch_size=64,             # 64 sentences per forward pass
    show_progress_bar=True,    # useful for long jobs
    normalize_embeddings=True, # L2-normalize output vectors
    convert_to_numpy=True,     # default; returns np.ndarray
)
print(f"Shape: {vectors.shape}")  # (100000, 384)

# To get a torch tensor instead:
vectors_torch = model.encode(
    documents[:100],
    convert_to_tensor=True,
    device="cpu",
)

when using models from the e5 or bge families — they are trained with normalized vectors and produce poor cosine similarities without it. For MiniLM and mpnet the library normalizes internally for similarity tasks, but explicit normalization never hurts and is the safe default.

from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
from sentence_transformers.util import cos_sim
import numpy as np

model = SentenceTransformer("all-MiniLM-L6-v2")

query = "What is retrieval-augmented generation?"
documents = [
    "RAG combines a retrieval step with a language model generator.",
    "Pasta carbonara is made with eggs, cheese, and guanciale.",
    "Vector databases store embeddings for similarity search.",
]

q_vec = model.encode([query], normalize_embeddings=True)
d_vecs = model.encode(documents, normalize_embeddings=True)

# cos_sim returns a tensor of shape (n_queries, n_docs)
scores = cos_sim(q_vec, d_vecs)
print(scores)
# tensor([[0.6821, 0.0312, 0.5143]])
# RAG document scores highest; pasta is near-zero (correctly)

# Alternatively, manual cosine with numpy (same result for normalized vecs):
scores_np = q_vec @ d_vecs.T  # dot product == cosine when normalized
print(scores_np)  # [[0.6821, 0.0312, 0.5143]]
Asymmetric Encoding: Query and Passage Prefixes

Some models are asymmetrically trained for retrieval: queries and documents go through the same model but with different text prefixes that shift the embedding. Using the wrong prefix (or no prefix) on an e5 or bge model degrades retrieval quality measurably. Always check the model card.
"Represent this sentence for searching relevant passages: "
best for CPU-only or latency-sensitive applications. Runs on a laptop with no GPU. Slightly lower quality than mpnet but often sufficient for general retrieval.
best English-only quality among symmetric models. Good default when you have a GPU and are indexing English text.
best choice for multilingual retrieval. Requires query/passage prefixes. Used by the cross-lingual RAG patterns covered in the cross-lingual topic.
state-of-the-art on MTEB multilingual retrieval. Also supports sparse retrieval (BM25-style) alongside dense embeddings. More complex setup but highest quality locally available.
Loading a model takes 1–5 seconds and allocates memory. In a web service, load the model once at startup and reuse the same instance across requests.
When to Use sentence-transformers vs. a Provider API
if documents cannot leave your network (GDPR-sensitive PII, classified data, HIPAA), sentence-transformers is the only option. No data is sent anywhere.
at roughly 100 million tokens per month, GPU inference cost drops below API pricing. Below that threshold, API simplicity usually wins.
sentence-transformers has a training API that lets you fine-tune any model on your own labeled pairs. Provider APIs do not offer this.
a local GPU call for 1 sentence takes ~2ms. A provider API call takes 50–200ms (network + server). For real-time applications, local wins.
A transformer encoder produces a contextualized embedding
. Mean pooling averages all token embeddings, weighted by the attention mask
. After normalization, cosine similarity equals the dot product:
This means normalized embeddings can be compared with matrix multiplication — enabling fast approximate nearest-neighbor search via FAISS without computing explicit cosine distances at query time.
sentence-transformers models are typically trained with Multiple Negatives Ranking (MNR) loss. Given a batch of
(query, positive) pairs, each query is contrasted against the other

is a temperature hyperparameter (typically 0.05). Larger batches provide more in-batch negatives, making each step more informative — which is why sentence-transformers models benefit from batch sizes of 512–8192 during training.

Dense embeddings are the foundational primitive for semantic search, RAG (retrieval-augmented generation), duplicate detection, and clustering of text. Without fast, accurate local embeddings, these applications require expensive API calls or custom model hosting.

At 10,000 documents, OpenAI's embedding API costs fractions of a dollar. At 100 million documents, the cost exceeds $10,000 — and API latency of 50–200ms per document makes batch indexing impractically slow. sentence-transformers on a single GPU indexes 100 million sentences in a few hours at under $100 compute cost.

For regulated industries, local embeddings are mandatory. Healthcare, legal, and financial applications under GDPR, HIPAA, or data-residency regulations cannot send document content to third-party APIs. sentence-transformers is the primary tool for privacy-sensitive embedding pipelines.
All computation happens locally. No document content leaves your infrastructure. Essential for GDPR, HIPAA, and sector-specific data-residency requirements.
No per-token or per-call billing. After the GPU cost of indexing, query-time embedding is effectively free — no cost per search query.
A local GPU call for one sentence takes ~2ms. A provider API call takes 50–200ms including network. For real-time search and recommendation, local wins decisively.

sentence-transformers provides a training API for fine-tuning on your own (query, positive, negative) triplets. Custom-tuned models improve retrieval NDCG by 10–30% on specialized corpora vs. general-purpose models.
3,000+ compatible models are available on the HuggingFace Hub, covering 100+ languages, specialized domains (legal, biomedical), and different size/quality trade-offs.
The library's encode() API handles tokenization, batching, attention masking, mean pooling, and normalization internally — one function call produces deployment-ready embeddings.
Managing GPU hardware, model downloads, CUDA versions, and memory requirements adds significant operational overhead compared to a provider API that abstracts all of this.
Quality ceiling below best provider APIs.
The best local models (bge-m3, multilingual-e5-large) score slightly below the best API-based models (Voyage voyage-3-large) on MTEB retrieval benchmarks — typically 2–4 NDCG points.
Loading a 560M-parameter model takes 3–8 seconds and requires 2–4 GB VRAM. In serverless or function-as-a-service environments, model loading latency is prohibitive without a warm pool.
Each encoder model has a max_seq_length (typically 256–512 tokens). Text beyond this limit is silently truncated. Provider APIs often support 8k–32k token inputs.
Embedding dimensions and similarity scores differ across models. Changing models invalidates all existing embeddings and requires re-indexing the entire corpus.

A legal document review platform must search 2 million contract clauses for semantic similarity to a query clause. Using a provider API at $0.13/M tokens would cost ~$500 per re-indexing, with each run taking 4+ hours due to API rate limits. Their local pipeline:

import numpy as np
import faiss
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer

model = SentenceTransformer("all-mpnet-base-v2", device="cuda")

def build_clause_index(clauses: list[str], index_path: str) -> None:
    """Embed 2M contract clauses and build a FAISS flat index."""
    embeddings = model.encode(
        clauses,
        batch_size=512,
        show_progress_bar=True,
        normalize_embeddings=True,
        convert_to_numpy=True,
    )  # shape: (N, 768)

    d = embeddings.shape[1]
    index = faiss.IndexFlatIP(d)   # inner product == cosine on normalized vecs
    index.add(embeddings.astype(np.float32))
    faiss.write_index(index, index_path)
    print(f"Indexed {index.ntotal} clauses.")


def search_clauses(
    query: str,
    index: faiss.Index,
    top_k: int = 10,
) -> list[tuple[int, float]]:
    q_vec = model.encode([query], normalize_embeddings=True).astype(np.float32)
    scores, indices = index.search(q_vec, top_k)
    return list(zip(indices[0].tolist(), scores[0].tolist()))


# 2M clauses on A100 80GB:
# Encoding time: ~25 minutes (batch_size=512)
# Index build: <2 minutes
# Query latency: <5ms (FAISS flat IP search over 2M vectors)
# Compute cost: ~$5 at spot GPU pricing
Result: 2 million clauses fully indexed in 27 minutes at ~$5 compute cost. Query latency under 5ms. Total cost for monthly re-indexing: ~$60 vs. ~$6,000/year API cost at the same volume.
Omitting query/passage prefixes for e5 and bge models.
e5 and bge model families require query: / passage: prefixes. Omitting them reduces NDCG@10 by 5–15 points on retrieval tasks. Always check the model card.
Loading the model inside every request.
Loading a model in every request takes 1–5 seconds. Use module-level initialization or lru_cache and reuse the instance across requests.

Without normalize_embeddings=True, cosine similarity requires explicit norm division. For e5 and bge models, the training objective assumes normalized vectors — skipping normalization produces poor similarity scores.
Mixing different models in the same vector index.
MiniLM (384-dim) and mpnet (768-dim) vectors are not interchangeable in a vector index. Different dimensions and different embedding spaces produce silently incorrect similarity scores.
Models have max_seq_length of 256–512 tokens. Longer text is silently truncated. Split long documents into overlapping chunks before encoding.
Setting batch_size without checking GPU memory.
Increasing batch_size beyond GPU memory causes OOM mid-batch with no helpful message. Start at batch_size=32 and scale up while monitoring VRAM.
Create one model instance and reuse it across requests. Use lru_cache or a module-level singleton to prevent repeated loading.
Always pass normalize_embeddings=True. Enables dot-product as cosine similarity, required for correct e5/bge comparisons, and compatible with FAISS IndexFlatIP.
Save embeddings to .npy files or a vector database after the first run. Recomputing 1M+ embeddings on each restart is expensive.
Choose the right model for your use case.
Use all-MiniLM-L6-v2 for CPU/latency-sensitive apps; all-mpnet-base-v2 for English quality on GPU; multilingual-e5-large or bge-m3 for multilingual retrieval.
Fine-tune on 1,000–5,000 domain-specific (query, positive) pairs using SentenceTransformerTrainer. Even small domain-specific datasets improve retrieval quality by 10–30%.
Maximize GPU throughput for large indexing jobs.
Set batch_size=256–512 on GPU and use show_progress_bar=True for large encoding jobs. Use convert_to_tensor=True when chaining with FAISS-GPU to avoid CPU-GPU transfer.
Throughput depends on model size, hardware, and batch size. Figures below are for encode() with normalize_embeddings=True.

For large corpora (>10M sentences), use encode_multi_process() which distributes encoding across multiple GPUs. For CPU inference, sentence-transformers automatically uses all available cores via PyTorch's thread parallelism. Set OMP_NUM_THREADS to the number of physical CPU cores for maximum throughput.

FAISS (Facebook AI Similarity Search) is the standard library for approximate nearest-neighbor search over dense embeddings. Supports flat (exact), IVF (partitioned), and HNSW indices. pip install faiss-cpu or faiss-gpu.

Managed vector databases that persist and search sentence-transformer embeddings with filtering, metadata, and horizontal scaling. ChromaDB is zero-configuration; Qdrant and Weaviate offer production-grade deployments.
The standard benchmark for comparing embedding models across 56 retrieval, clustering, classification, and reranking tasks. Use to select the best model for your task type.

Cross-encoders (also from the sentence-transformers library) rerank top-K candidates from a bi-encoder retrieval pass. They are slower but more accurate for re-ranking. Use as a second stage after initial retrieval with a bi-encoder.
LangChain HuggingFaceEmbeddings and LlamaIndex HuggingFaceEmbedding both wrap sentence-transformers for use in RAG pipeline orchestration.
For multi-vector and late-interaction retrieval (ColBERT), Pylate provides a complementary approach that sometimes outperforms single-vector bi-encoders on complex retrieval tasks.
encode() silently truncates text exceeding max_seq_length. For document retrieval, long contracts lose their tail content. Always chunk and verify no critical information is truncated.

The first call to SentenceTransformer() downloads the model from HuggingFace Hub. In air-gapped environments, pre-download models during image build and load from local paths: SentenceTransformer('/models/all-MiniLM-L6-v2'). Pin library versions.

Embeddings encode the semantic content of text. Approximate inversion attacks can recover approximate text from embeddings for short inputs. Treat embedding storage with the same access controls as raw documents for sensitive content.
Different sentence-transformers versions may produce slightly different vectors for the same model. Pin the library version to guarantee reproducibility across re-indexing runs.
GPU memory isolation in multi-tenant deployments.
On multi-tenant GPU servers, concurrent encode() calls share GPU memory. Set per-process batch_size limits and use process-level model isolation to prevent OOM from one workload killing others.
sentence-transformers wraps transformer encoders with mean pooling to produce fixed-size vectors where cosine similarity measures semantic relatedness.
Always use normalize_embeddings=True — it enables fast dot-product similarity and is required for correct scores with e5 and bge model families.
e5 and bge models require query: / passage: prefixes; symmetric models (MiniLM, mpnet) need no prefix. Wrong prefix degrades retrieval quality by 5–15 NDCG points.
Load the model once per process with lru_cache or module-level initialization — model loading takes 1–5 seconds and must not happen per request.
At ~100M tokens/month, local GPU inference costs less than provider APIs and removes per-token billing, network latency, and data-residency concerns.
Fine-tune on domain-specific (query, positive) pairs using SentenceTransformerTrainer for 10–30% retrieval improvement on specialized corpora.
For large indexing jobs, use batch_size=256–512 on GPU with show_progress_bar=True; cache the resulting embeddings to avoid recomputing on restart.
